review_summary: The transcript provides enough grounded detail to generate 6 reinforcement questions about tokenizer mechanics, AutoTokenizer, common padding and decoding utilities, and one short code check.

question_bank.question: What does a tokenizer primarily produce for a transformer model?
choices: Numerical inputs such as input IDs and attention masks
A final class prediction only
A learned optimizer and scheduler
Gradient updates for model weights
question_bank.explanation: The tokenizer is the preprocessing step that turns raw text into model-usable numerical representations such as input IDs and attention masks.

question_bank.question: Why is AutoTokenizer especially useful?
choices: It automatically loads the tokenizer aligned with the chosen model
It always makes tokenization language-independent
It removes the need for special tokens
It guarantees shorter sequences than any manual tokenizer
question_bank.explanation: AutoTokenizer helps avoid mismatches by selecting the tokenizer that corresponds to the chosen model and its token-to-index mapping.

question_bank.question: What is the usual default when using AutoTokenizer?
choices: The fast Rust tokenizer
The pure Python tokenizer
A character-level tokenizer
No tokenizer is loaded automatically
question_bank.explanation: The workflow notes that the fast tokenizer, implemented in Rust, is generally the default when using AutoTokenizer.

question_bank.question: What is the correct high-level order of the tokenization pipeline?
choices: Split text into tokens, convert tokens to IDs, then add required special tokens
Add special tokens, convert raw characters to logits, then split into tokens
Convert text directly to labels, then pad it, then decode it
Decode text into strings, then split into tokens, then remove IDs
question_bank.explanation: The tokenizer first breaks text into tokens, then maps those tokens to IDs, and finally adds any model-required special tokens for inference.

question_bank.question: When batching different-length strings, what do padding and truncation help with?
choices: They normalize sequence lengths so batched tensors can be formed cleanly
They convert labels into class logits
They replace the need for attention masks
They force every sentence to use the same vocabulary
question_bank.explanation: Padding makes shorter examples reach a common length, while truncation shortens overly long examples, which makes batching practical.

question_bank.question: What does this Hugging Face tokenization call guarantee about the batch?
choices: All tokenized sequences are padded to the same length in the returned batch
Every sequence is truncated to length 1
The tokenizer returns raw strings instead of ids
The model is run automatically after tokenization
question_bank.explanation: Padding makes sequences in the same batch share a common length so they can be packed into one tensor.

code_block.snippet:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
batch = tok(["hello world", "hi"], padding=True, return_tensors="pt")
